Title: Ceasefire relief lifts Bursa despite oil volatility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia ended 0.66 per cent higher on Thursday as extended ceasefire in the Middle East eased market jitters, lifting investor sentiment even as Brent crude oil prices remained elevated.
The benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI climbed steadily through the day, supported by sustained buying in index-heavyweights particularly banking and industrial products counters, following news of an extension to the US-Iran ceasefire.
The index gained 0.41 per cent, or 7.08 points, to close the morning session at 1,717.47, before extending its gains into the afternoon.
By 5pm, it had risen 0.66 per cent, or 11.31 points, to settle at 1,721.70.
Over the past five trading days, the FBM KLCI has advanced 1.66 per cent, bringing its year-to-date gain to 2.44 per cent, reflecting improving risk appetite despite ongoing global uncertainties.
Analysts said the market rally was driven largely by relief buying, as investors reacted positively to signs of de-escalation in geopolitical tensions that had previously weighed on sentiment.
However, they cautioned that volatility in crude oil prices could continue to influence near-term market direction, particularly for energy-sensitive sectors and inflation-linked plays.
Tradeview Capital fund manager Neoh Jia Man told Business Times that while the financial services sector remains the main focus for foreign institutional investors, recent buying activity may also partly be driven by yield-seeking domestic institutions.
This trend could be linked to the removal of the preferential withholding tax rate for real estate investment trusts.
Neoh said most of the day's gains were driven by relief following the announced ceasefire, but cautioned that with oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz still restricted, the fundamental earnings outlook remains uncertain.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said the index was lifted in line with Wall Street, which recorded a third straight record close.
A strong start to the US earnings season helped investors look past rising oil prices and refocus on corporate fundamentals and forward guidance.
Sedek added that the earnings-driven resilience has provided a constructive backdrop for regional markets, with local investors shifting attention away from oil-related concerns towards earnings momentum as the key near-term support for equities.
"That said, this shift reflects a tactical repricing rather than a resolution of underlying risks.
"There remains little evidence of a durable end to current geopolitical tensions, with both sides continuing a broader contest of signals and rhetoric, leaving oil risk firmly in play.
"Sustained prices above US$100 per barrel could increasingly feed through into inflation expectations, cost pressures, and margin compression. In our view, market resilience remains fragile," he said.
In the current environment, Sedek said, a selective approach is still warranted, with emphasis on sectors and companies that have stronger earnings visibility and pricing power.
He added that the sustainability of the market's strength will depend on how energy markets and geopolitical developments unfold.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Keeping your composure: Handling provocation at work
Article:
WORKPLACES bring together a wide range of personalities, and not all interactions will be smooth.
Occasionally, you may encounter individuals who speak carelessly, spread nonsense, or deliberately try to provoke you into conflict. Being goaded for a reaction can feel deeply frustrating, especially when it seems undeserved.
Yet, how you respond in these moments often matters more than the provocation itself. Maintaining composure is not about weakness; it is about control, clarity, and long-term credibility.
The first step is recognising what is actually happening. People who "talk rubbish" or provoke others are often seeking attention, validation or a sense of power.
Their behaviour may stem from insecurity, stress, or a lack of awareness. Understanding this does not excuse their actions, but it helps you avoid taking their words personally.
When you see provocation as a tactic rather than a truth, it becomes easier to detach emotionally.
Emotional control is your greatest asset in such situations. A reactive response, especially one driven by anger, tends to escalate matters. It gives the provocateur exactly what they want: a visible reaction.
Instead, pause before responding. A moment of silence, a steady breath, or even choosing not to reply immediately can shift the dynamic.
Composure communicates confidence and denies the other person the satisfaction of disrupting you.
Equally important is choosing your battles wisely. Not every comment deserves a response.
Some remarks are best ignored, particularly if they are trivial or designed purely to irritate.
Engaging with every provocation can drain your energy and distract you from your priorities. By being selective, you maintain focus and demonstrate maturity.
Silence, when used intentionally, can be more powerful than any retort.
However, ignoring does not mean tolerating persistent disrespect.
When behaviour crosses a line or becomes repetitive, it is appropriate to address it calmly and directly. The key is to respond without hostility.
Use clear, measured language and focus on the behaviour rather than the person. For example, stating that a comment is unhelpful or inappropriate keeps the discussion grounded and professional. This approach sets boundaries without escalating tension.
Another effective strategy is to ask neutral, clarifying questions. When someone makes a provocative or nonsensical remark, inviting them to explain can shift the burden back onto them.
Questions such as "What do you mean by that?" or "Can you clarify your point?" often expose the weakness of their argument. This method keeps the conversation rational and discourages further provocation.
It is also worth considering the broader context. Workplace dynamics, power structures, and existing relationships all influence how situations unfold.
If the person provoking you holds a position of authority or influence, discretion becomes even more important.
In such cases, documenting incidents and seeking guidance through appropriate channels may be necessary. Handling the matter quietly and professionally protects both your reputation and your position.
Support systems play a valuable role as well. Speaking to a trusted colleague or mentor can provide perspective and reassurance. They may help you assess whether you are dealing with a one-off incident or a pattern of behaviour.
External viewpoints can prevent you from overreacting while also ensuring you do not dismiss something that requires attention.
Self-awareness is equally crucial. Provocation often works because it taps into personal sensitivities.
Reflecting on what triggers you can help you prepare for future encounters. When you understand your own responses, you are better equipped to manage them. This is not about suppressing emotions but about directing them constructively.
Maintaining professionalism at all times is essential. Even when others behave poorly, your conduct should remain consistent.
Reputation in the workplace is built over time, and moments of conflict can either strengthen or damage it. By staying calm, measured and respectful, you demonstrate reliability and integrity.
These qualities are often recognised and valued, even if not immediately.
It is also helpful to focus on your purpose.
Workplaces are, ultimately, environments for achieving goals and contributing meaningfully.
Allowing yourself to be drawn into unnecessary conflict detracts from this purpose. Keeping your attention on your responsibilities and objectives provides a steady anchor.
It reminds you that not every distraction deserves your time or energy.
In some cases, humour can be a subtle and effective tool. A light, non-confrontational response can defuse tension and signal that you are not easily rattled.
However, this approach requires careful judgement. Humour should never belittle or escalate the situation, and it is best used when you are confident it will be received appropriately.
Finally, know when to step away. If a conversation becomes unproductive or overly heated, it is entirely reasonable to disengage.
Politely ending the interaction preserves your composure and prevents further escalation. Walking away is not avoidance; it is a deliberate choice to prioritise professionalism and well-being.
Dealing with people who provoke or speak irresponsibly is an inevitable part of working life.
Yet, it also presents an opportunity to demonstrate resilience, discipline, and emotional intelligence.
By staying composed, setting clear boundaries, and focusing on what truly matters, you not only protect yourself but also reinforce a culture of respect.
In the end, your response defines you far more than their provocation ever could.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 250 Indians claiming descent from biblical tribe land in Israel
Article:
TEL AVIV: More than 250 Indians claiming descent from a biblical tribe landed at Tel Aviv airport on Thursday, as part of a government operation to relocate them to Israel.
The newcomers passed under a balloon arch in blue and white, the colours of the Israeli flag, as dozens of well-wishers welcomed them with a traditional Jewish song.
They were the first "Bnei Menashe" ("Sons of Manasseh") to arrive in Israel since the government decided in November to fund the immigration of around 4,600 members of the community from the state of Manipur in northeast India.
The community claims to descend from Manasseh, the forefather of one of the biblical "lost tribes" of Israel exiled in 720 BC by Assyrian conquerors.
Dagan Zolat, who has lived in Israel for 20 years, is at the airport to meet a man who he describes as his brother.
"We were neighbours and among the only Jews in our village," he told AFP, adding that it was nine years since he had seen his friend.
[[nid:1424830]]
"When my son was little (in India), my friend often carried him in his arms," he said.
The Shavei Israel organisation, which seeks to trace the descendants of the lost tribes, said that some 4,000 Bnei Menashe have already immigrated to Israel since the 1990s, with around 7,000 others still living in India.
Their oral history tells of a centuries-long exodus through Persia, Afghanistan, Tibet and China, all the while adhering to certain Jewish religious practices, like circumcision.
In India, they were converted to Christianity by 19th-century missionaries.
The 250 Bnei Menashe who arrived on Thursday are to settle in northern Israel, according to the ministry of integration.
They will need to convert in order to become Israeli citizens.
Immigration Minister Ofir Sofer, who welcomed the newcomers at the airport, told AFP that their arrival marked a "historic moment".
"This is the beginning of an operation that will allow the entire community to immigrate, 1,200 per year," he said.
Manipur has seen periodic clashes for nearly three years between the predominantly Hindu Meitei majority and the mainly Christian Kuki community that have killed more than 250 people.
Since April 2025, more than 18,000 Jews have immigrated to Israel, down 18 percent over the previous year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel, Lebanon extend ceasefire as Trump hopes for historic deal
Article:
WASHINGTON: Israel and Lebanon on Thursday extended a shaky ceasefire by three weeks, President Donald Trump announced Thursday, as he voiced hope for a historic three-way meeting soon and a potential peace deal.
Trump, at a standstill in negotiations with Iran, spoke in glowing terms of peace prospects for Lebanon, even as Hizbollah fired new rockets following deadly Israeli strikes.
"I think there's a very good chance of having peace. I think it should be an easy one," Trump told reporters as he met in the White House with ambassadors of the two countries, which have no diplomatic relations.
Trump announced that a ceasefire would be extended by three weeks. An initial truce was announced after the first meeting between the ambassadors on April 14 and was due to expire on Sunday.
He said that he expected Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun to meet jointly with him at the White House during the ceasefire.
The two leaders "over the next couple of weeks will be coming here," Trump said.
Aoun earlier denied an account by Trump that he would speak by telephone with Netanyahu.
Lebanese officials said before the meeting that they were seeking a one-month ceasefire extension. Aoun said it should include "a halt to the destruction of homes and attacks on civilians, places of worship, journalists and the medical and educational sectors."
Israel last month launched a major attack on Lebanon, killing over 2,450 people and displacing one million, according to authorities.
The offensive came in response to fire by Hizbollah, which has vowed to avenge Israel's killing of Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, at the start of the US-Israel war on February 28.
Israel's ambassador to Washington, Yechiel Leiter, said that his country wanted a peace deal with the Lebanese government and believed Hizbollah has been weakened by the attacks on Iran's cleric-run state.
"We're united with the Lebanese government in wanting to rid the country of this malign influence called Hizbollah," he said.
The two countries have been at war for decades and until last week had not met so directly since 1993.
NEW VIOLENCE DESPITE TRUCE
Just as the ambassadors were meeting with Trump, Hizbollah announced that it had fired rockets at northern Israel "in response to the Israeli enemy's violation of the ceasefire."
Israel said that the launches were intercepted.
Israeli strikes killed five people in Lebanon on Wednesday, as Israel continues to hit what it says are Hizbollah targets despite the ceasefire.
Lebanon's state-run National News Agency on Thursday reported an Israeli drone strike on a vehicle near Nabatieh, around 35 kilometers (22 miles) north of the border with Israel.
Under the truce terms, Israel says it reserves the right to act against "planned, imminent or ongoing attacks."
Hizbollah also claimed at least two attacks on Israeli troops in southern Lebanon and said it targeted a reconnaissance drone.
Among the dead on Wednesday was a Lebanese newspaper journalist, Amal Khalil.
Israeli troops have also taken over southern Lebanese villages in a 10-kilometer (six-mile) deep "security zone" from its border.
Iran had demanded the truce in Lebanon as a condition to resume talks with Washington on a permanent end to weeks of war.
Iran refused to attend a second round of talks with the United States this week due to an ongoing US naval blockade.
But Trump extended a truce with Iran indefinitely. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US slaps sanctions on Cambodian senator and others over alleged scam network
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States imposed sanctions Thursday on a Cambodian senator, alongside people and entities in his network, charging that they have been "defrauding Americans" at scam compounds across the Southeast Asian country.
Senator Kok An and his affiliates were said to have operated "out of casinos and office parks retrofitted for fraudulent activity," laundered victims' funds and provided a base to target US citizens, the US Treasury Department added in a statement.
Besides him, 28 people and entities were also targetted by the Treasury.
Kok An runs a business called Crown Resorts, which owns casinos and other buildings in Cambodian cities, the department said.
The US State Department charged in a separate statement that "Kok An and his associates have operated and profited from scam compounds that target US citizens and often use human trafficking victims who are forced to commit unlawful acts under the threat of violence."
Separately, the US Justice Department announced charges against two people said to have managed a cryptocurrency investment fraud compound in Myanmar and who tried to start another complex in Cambodia.
The State Department meanwhile announced rewards for information leading to the seizure or recovery of proceeds linked to "Tai Chang" scam centers in Myanmar said to be conducting online fraud.
Cambodia and Myanmar have emerged as hotspots for cyber scam operations in recent years. Transnational crime groups initially mostly targeted Chinese speakers before widening their reach and stealing tens of billions of dollars annually from victims around the world.
Organised criminal gangs have used casinos, hotels and fortified compounds in Southeast Asian countries as bases to carry out sophisticated online scams, defrauding people through cryptocurrency investment schemes and fake romantic relationships, according to the UN Office on Drugs and Crime.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: First glimpse of RMAF's FA-50M jet
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
The Royal Malaysian Air Force (RMAF) has offered the first glimpse of its new fleet of FA-50M light combat aircraft, with pictures of the air force chief at the assembly plant in South Korea posted on social media.
RMAF chief General Datuk Seri Muhamad Norazlan Aris posed with an aircraft marked "01" during a working visit to Korea Aerospace Industries (KAI) in Sacheon on April 12-15.
He was also briefed on the progress of the FA-50M fleet's assembly ahead of the expected delivery next year.
"The briefing covered key aspects, including aircraft development, system integration and delivery timelines to ensure the programme remains on schedule.
"During the visit, Norazlan also conducted a physical inspection of the aircraft assembly and system integration process at KAI's facilities," said the air force.
Norazlan also held discussions with KAI on future upgrade programmes, while identifying implementation challenges and how to mitigate them, to ensure the smooth execution of the acquisition.
"The FA-50M programme is a key component of RMAF's capability enhancement plans.
"The visit reflects RMAF's continued emphasis on ensuring that the development and acquisition of new assets are carried out in a structured and comprehensive manner, in line with its focus on achieving high levels of capability and readiness, as well as sustaining air defence assets to meet evolving security challenges," it said.
In May 2023, Malaysia and KAI signed a contract worth almost RM4 billion for 18 FA-50M aircraft.
The FA-50Ms will replace the ageing BAE Systems Hawk 108/208 aircraft, which have been in service since 1995.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bangladeshi killed in MEX drink-driving crash was major gaming influencer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Muzahid Millad, the Bangladeshi tourist killed in a suspected drink-driving crash early yesterday has been identified by his fans as gaming content creator Advance Gaming.
News of his death triggered an outpouring of grief and calls for justice across Bangladeshi social media.
Bangladesh's online gaming community circulated hashtags such as #JusticeForMuzahid, #JusticeForAdvanceGaming, #BangladeshDemandsJustice and #advancegaming as calls for accountability grew over the fatal crash.
Muzahid, 22, was killed when the e-hailing vehicle carrying him and his wife from Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) was struck on the MEX Highway.
His wife, Nafisa Tabassum Adiba, 23, was injured and is receiving treatment at Kuala Lumpur Hospital.
The couple, from Habiganj, had arrived in Malaysia for holiday and were planning to spend a week in Kuala Lumpur.
A post on Muzahid's YouTube channel, Advance Gaming, which has more than 777,000 subscribers, read: "With deep sorrow, it is being shared that Advance Gaming, or Muzahid, passed away this morning (April 23) following an accident in Malaysia.
"Advance Gaming was a content creator who made many videos. If any of his words ever hurt anyone, may he be forgiven. Please pray for him and may Allah grant him paradise."
The post drew a wave of condolences in the comment section.
Tribute posts and videos linked to Muzahid were also visible online, reflecting the shock among his followers over his death.
Yesterday, the New Straits Times reported that
police had arrested a 31-year-old soldier
on suspicion of driving under the influence of alcohol and causing the fatal accident.
The case is being investigated under Section 44(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for driving under the influence of alcohol and causing death, an offence punishable by up to 15 years' jail and a fine of up to RM100,000.
Nafisa's uncle, Mohibul Hassan, had also
called for justice
, questioning how a drink-driver could have been allowed on the road and expressing hope that Malaysian law would take its course.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Khaled Nordin, meanwhile, had
confirmed he had been made aware
that a soldier was involved in the fatal crash, adding however that the ministry and the military would leave it to police to investigate.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PayNet sees surge in digital transactions
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Payments Network Malaysia (PayNet) processed 8.44 billion digital transactions in 2025, marking a significant leap toward a cashless society.
Spanning retail, transportation, peer-to-peer transfers and small-business payments, the transactions reflect how digital payments are becoming embedded in daily life.
Chief executive officer Praveen Rajan said the growth signals a broader shift in how Malaysians move and manage money.
"Digital payments are becoming the preferred method for consumers, businesses and public services. This is driven by innovation and collaboration among banks, non-bank players, merchants and the government."
PayNet said growth in 2025 was driven by increased participation, as banks and non-bank players expanded access for consumers and merchants.
Bank transaction volumes rose 30.69 per cent, while non-bank transactions surged 71.7 per cent, indicating a more diversified and inclusive payments landscape.
This resulted in higher activity, with an additional 6.3 million transactions per day compared with 2024.
Volumes were also stronger during festive periods.
As volumes rise, maintaining system reliability and user trust remains critical.
PayNet's network processed an average of 260 transactions per second, while maintaining 99.995 per cent service availability.
Praveen said: "As digital payments grow, trust and security remain critical. Strengthening safeguards against scams while maintaining a resilient, accessible infrastructure will be a priority."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: USS George H.W. Bush aircraft carrier in Mideast region
Article:
WASHINGTON: The USS George H.W. Bush aircraft carrier has arrived in the Middle East, the US military said Thursday, bringing the number of the massive American warships operating in the region to three.
The Bush was sailing "in the Indian Ocean in the US Central Command area of responsibility, April 23," the military command responsible for the Middle East said in a post on X that included an image of the carrier with its deck packed with warplanes.
A second carrier – the USS Gerald R. Ford – was operating in the Red Sea on Thursday, while a third – the USS Abraham Lincoln – is also in the region, according to social media posts by CENTCOM.
The deployment of the third aircraft carrier to the Middle East comes during a more than two-week ceasefire that paused the devastating US-Israeli air campaign against Iran that was launched in late February.
The Ford sailed to Croatia for repairs several weeks ago after a laundry fire broke out aboard the ship on March 12, but the world's largest aircraft carrier has since returned to operations.
The Ford has been at sea for more about 10 months – a deployment that has already seen it take part in US operations in the Caribbean, where Washington's forces have carried out strikes on alleged drug-smuggling boats, interdicted sanctioned tankers and seized Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro.
The aircraft carriers each travel with substantial groups of supporting warships.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eradicate scam centres, China urges Cambodia
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
CHINA's top diplomat Wang Yi called for the complete eradication of scam centres in Cambodia during a meeting with Prime Minister Hun Manet in Phnom Penh on Wednesday, according to Beijing's foreign ministry.
Cambodia hosts dozens of scam centres with tens of thousands of people perpetrating online scams — some willingly and others trafficked — in a multibillion-dollar illicit industry, say rights monitors.
But under pressure from several countries, including China, Cambodian authorities say they are cracking down on the industry.
"Cross-border gambling and fraud endanger the lives and property of the people and must be resolutely cracked down on and completely eradicated," Wang told Hun Manet, according to China's foreign ministry.
China and Cambodia maintain close trade, diplomatic and military ties.
Wang, who was accompanied by Defence Minister Dong Jun, also underlined the two countries' "unbreakable bond" during the meeting.
Hun Manet said in February scam centres were destroying his country's economy and giving the nation a bad name, vowing to "clean this out".
On Wednesday, he wrote on social media that he, Wang and Dong had discussed promoting cooperation in politics, trade and investment, national defence and security, clean energy, transportation infrastructure construction and agriculture.
Wang and Dong also met Cambodia's Foreign Minister Prak Sokhonn and Defence Minister Tea Seiha.
The Chinese foreign minister said he supported initiatives aimed at normalising relations between Cambodia and Thailand following deadly clashes last year along their shared border.
"China is willing to continue to build more platforms for the resumption of exchanges and direct dialogue between Cambodia and Thailand," said Wang.
While a ceasefire was agreed to in December, the regional situation remains fragile, with Cambodia and Thailand accusing each other of failing to respect the truce. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran's Mojtaba Khamenei gravely wounded but mentally sharp: NYT
Article:
WASHINGTON: Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei was seriously wounded in the US-Israeli airstrike that killed his father and predecessor Ali Khamenei but is mentally sharp, the New York Times reported on Thursday.
Citing several Iranian officials which it did not name, the Times said Mojtaba Khamenei had "at least for now" delegated decision-making to generals in the Revolutionary Guards ideological army.
Mojtaba Khamenei has not appeared in public since succeeding his father and only issued written statements, creating speculation over his condition and if he is still alive.
Although Mojtaba Khamenei was "gravely wounded (in the Feb 28 airstrike), he is mentally sharp and engaged," said the NYT.
"One leg was operated on three times, and he is awaiting a prosthetic. He had surgery on one hand and is slowly regaining function. His face and lips have been burned severely, making it difficult for him to speak," it cited the officials as saying, adding that "eventually, he will need plastic surgery."
Access for security reasons is extremely limited to Khamenei, who remains in hiding, with only handwritten messages passed on, it said.
Guards commanders do not visit him but President Masoud Pezeshkian, who is also a heart surgeon, has been involved in his care, it said.
It said the generals from the Guards who viewed the war with the US and Israel "as a threat to the regime's survival" that has now been contained.
They have also been in charge of military strategy, including the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: In war-torn Gaza, a small election carries big political meaning
Article:
Palestinians in one of the only Gaza cities not overrun by Israeli ground forces during the war will vote this weekend in municipal elections that will feature some pro-Hamas candidates, offering a rare barometer of the resistance group's popularity.
The vote in Deir al-Balah is part of Palestinian Authority (PA) municipal elections that Palestinians have cast as a display of national unity against a United States plan for Gaza that they believe intends to entrench their separation from the occupied West Bank.
It will be Gaza's first vote of any kind since 2006, when Hamas won the PA's legislative elections and later seized control of Gaza following a brief civil war with PA President Mahmoud Abbas' Fatah party, dominant in the West Bank.
The polls will be the fifth municipal elections in the West Bank since 2005.
In January, the PA said it would extend those elections to Gaza "wherever possible", a move analysts see as a symbolic effort to show Gaza remains part of a future Palestinian state.
For Palestinians in Deir al-Balah, including Adham Al-Bardini, tomorrow's vote marks an opportunity for political expression after the Hamas-led attacks of Oct 7, 2023 that sparked Israel's devastating two-year assault on Gaza.
"For the first time in my life, in 20 years, I will have this feeling. I have been hearing about elections since I was born but because of the circumstances no elections are done," said Al-Bardini, 34. "We are eager to take (part) so we can change the reality imposed on us."
Voting will be held in 12 polling centres including open fields and tents.
Fareed Taamallah, spokesman for the PA Central Elections Commission, said roughly 70,000 Palestinians were eligible to vote. Deir al-Balah was chosen because it suffered less damage than the rest of the largely ruined territory.
Four lists are fielding candidates in the election, including one that has several candidates regarded as pro-Hamas.
Hamas has not explicitly fielded a list or endorsed any candidate, citing disagreements with Abbas over a PA decree that requires candidates to accept terms including recognition of Israel.
Other factions are also boycotting the vote, meaning Fatah is expected to sweep larger city councils in the West Bank.
But despite its official boycott of the vote, Hamas "may be betting on winning in this election" and could use pro-Hamas candidates' performance to gauge its popularity.
Hamas spokesman Hazem Qassem said the group would respect the election results. Sources in the group said it will deploy police and security forces to secure voting sites.
Hamas reasserted control of Deir al-Balah and other areas in a stretch of Gaza's coast from which Israeli forces withdrew under an October 2025 ceasefire.
Israel retains control of over 53 per cent of Gaza.
Some public opinion polling shows Hamas remains popular in Gaza and the West Bank, despite the devastation wrought by the war.
In Gaza, an October 2025 poll by the Palestinian Centre for Policy and Survey Research found that 41 per cent of Palestinians there supported Hamas, followed by 29 per cent for Fatah.
The vote comes as US President Donald Trump's so-called "Board of Peace" pushes a plan for Gaza's future that would see the territory rebuilt from scratch under the administration of an apolitical committee of Palestinian technocrats.
The plan calls on Hamas to hand over Gaza's governance to the committee as it lays down its weapons and Israeli forces withdraw from the Strip.
Hamas has rejected disarmament, accusing Israel of failing to abide by the October ceasefire.
The plan notably does not mention the West Bank, which, along with the Gaza Strip, Palestinians have long sought for a future state, and where the PA exercises limited self-rule.
Palestinian political analyst Reham Owda said the municipal elections were "a symbolic step to send a message to the world, to the Board of Peace, and to Israel that the Gaza Strip is an inseparable part of the Palestinian political system".
For 25-year-old Abdul-Rahman Al-Shaaf, the vote, even at the local level, offers an opportunity to rebuild lives after conflict.
"Everyone wants to improve the country, especially after two years of war, what we witnessed, and the destruction," he said.
THE WRITERS ARE FROM REUTERS
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anzac Day: Remembering those who stood with Malaya in its darkest hours
Article:
Every April 25, Australia and New Zealand mark Anzac Day in memory of the 11,000 young men who lost their lives in the Battle of Gallipoli in 1915.
Their bravery, endurance and loyalty, live on in the Anzac spirit, and it is this spirit that has come to symbolise Anzac Day.
For Malaysia, Anzac Day is when we remember the Australians and Kiwis who made the ultimate sacrifice in the Malayan Emergency of 1948-1960, and then the Confrontation of 1963-1966.
Ours is not a bond forged in a boardroom or through a trade agreement; it was tempered in the humid, emerald depths of the Malayan jungle.
Though the Anzac troops came to the Malayan shores as part of the Commonwealth effort in 1948 to stave off the communist threat, they stayed on even after independence in 1957 to fight in the counterinsurgency operations of the Malayan Emergency.
Thousands of young men from across the Tasman Sea answered a call to protect a fledgling nation.
They came again in 1963 when Confrontation was launched against Malaysia and bore witness to the new federation following the United Nations referendum.
What remains decades later is a profound sense of  "terima kasih" that transcends borders.
For the young Anzac soldiers arriving in the 1950s, Malaya was a sensory shock.
They traded the dry plains of Australia and the rolling hills of New Zealand for a world of perpetual twilight beneath the jungle canopy.
The "Emergency" was a gruelling guerilla war against the military arm of the Malayan Communist Party.
The experience was defined by a waiting game — soldiers spent weeks on patrol, moving through mud that swallowed boots and humidity that turned uniforms into a second, itchy skin.
They faced leeches, malaria, and the  constant psychological strain of an invisible enemy.
The contribution was significant and sustained, and its cost was paid in suffering and in lives.
Their deep sacrifice was for a land that was not their own, and a military operation that was not theirs to fight.
In World War 2, of the 2,434 British and Australian prisoners of war forced to build an airstrip in Sandakan's rugged interior, only six — all Australians — survived.
Those who did not make it are forever remembered at the Sandakan Memorial Park in Malaysia, every Aug 15.
Those who fell as Malaysia was becoming a Federation in 1963 are remembered at the Terendak Military Cemetery in Melaka, honouring those who stayed behind forever.
What made the Anzac presence unique was the "hearts and minds" campaign. Soldiers didn't just patrol; they built clinics, repaired bridges and shared their rations with hungry children.
Australians and Kiwis became a familiar sight in the local markets, known for their easy-going nature and willingness to play a game of football with the local youth.
Seventy years on, Malaysia does not forget. On Anzac Day, ceremonies at the Tugu Negara in Kuala Lumpur are attended by high-ranking Malaysian officials and ordinary citizens alike.
There is a deep recognition that without the Anzac intervention, the Malaysian trajectory towards a peaceful, multi-ethnic democracy might have been derailed by radical insurgency.
In 2016, there was the repatriation of several fallen soldiers back to Australia. The ceremony was conducted with the highest military honours, showing that even 50 years later, the debt of gratitude remained fresh.
"They came when we were young and vulnerable," a retired Malaysian brigadier-general once remarked.
"They didn't just bring guns; they brought a sense of security that allowed us to build a nation. They are part of our story now."
The legacy of  the Anzac heroes in Malaya is a testament to the idea that peace is a collaborative effort.
The "Diggers" and "Kiwis" who trekked through the vine-choked ridges of the peninsula left behind more than just tactical victories; they left a blueprint for international friendship.
As the sun sets over the Straits of Malacca, the echoes of those jungle patrols fade. Yet, in the quiet corners of the country, the names of the fallen are still whispered with respect.
Australia and New Zealand didn't just fight for a piece of land; they fought for the right of a people to choose their own future. And for that, the heart of Malaysia remains forever grateful.
THE WRITER IS A FOREIGN SERVICE OFFICER WHO HAS SERVED IN BILATERAL AND MULTILATERAL POSTS
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times

Title: Fate of Malaysian detained in Japan to be decided tomorrow
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The fate of Muhammad Faiq Zafran Mohd Jailani, 24, who is currently detained in Toyamanishi, Japan, will be known at his final hearing tomorrow at the Toyama Summary Court.
The lawyer appointed by his family, Datuk Noor Svetlana Mohd Noor Nordin, said the outcome would depend on submissions made by both the defence and prosecution on April 16.
She said Faiq was fortunate that his father was present during the earlier hearing, which helped in mitigation by the defence.
"He has been detained there for more than six months, and claims circulating that he has already pleaded guilty are not true.
"Tomorrow will be the second day of his trial following the first hearing on April 16. Claims by some parties that he pleaded guilty in February are entirely inaccurate.
"I have also met the legal aid lawyer to obtain an accurate update. However, as I am not registered to practise in that country, my role and efforts are limited," she said when contacted today.
She said she would attend the hearing together with Faiq's father, Mohd Jailani, to monitor proceedings.
It was previously reported that Faiq, who travelled to Japan in November last year, was believed to have fallen victim to an online scam syndicate operating in the country, which led to his detention in Toyama.
https://www.nst.com.my/news/nation/2026/02/1379796/wisma-putra-consular-officers-ready-assist-malaysian-detained-japan
There were also conflicting reports regarding his arrest, including early claims that he was detained with cash and gold worth about RM500,000.
Faiq is being investigated under Article 246(1) of Japan's Penal Code for fraud, as well as Article 60 of the same code for offences committed jointly, which are similar to Sections 420 and 34 of Malaysia's Penal Code.
Noor Svetlana said that after the charges were read on April 16, Faiq, who appeared downcast, pleaded guilty.
"Before the court proceedings on April 16, we were unable to speak with him at all.
"However, I hope there will be a positive outcome tomorrow and that a lenient sentence will be imposed. If a heavy sentence is handed down, it will be difficult for him to return home soon.
"I have also appealed to the legal aid lawyer to assist as much as possible, as the family is not well-off. His father is an ambulance driver, while his mother is a homemaker," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: Growing old, growing isolated
Article:
A noble cause that most Malaysians honour,  regardless of the circumstances,  is caring for the elderly, particularly our own parents.
This filial piety  is a cultural value deeply entrenched within our multiethnic society. Rooted in religious and historical philosophies, the tradition of caring for elders fosters a reciprocal bond of psychological strength, medical care, and overall well-being.
Yet, this time-honoured system has in recent years come under severe strain. Government data indicates that Malaysia's elderly population has reached 11.6 per cent, meaning roughly four million are now aged 60 or older.
Of these, 36 per cent live alone, while many others face the spectre of loneliness. While the issue is not solely due to an ageing society, it is certainly  attributable to weakening traditional family structures.
If the tradition of filial piety remains strong, why are there increasing reports of  the elderly living, and even dying, alone?
The causes undoubtedly lie in rapid urbanisation, economic pressures and changing family dynamics. As adult children are forced to  migrate to cities for work,  their parents are often left in isolation; although many elders embrace  self-reliance and rely on financial  stipends to avoid  burdening their children.
These days, this loneliness is increasingly mitigated by seniors   adopting smartphones and social media to maintain contact.
For others, loneliness is a daily shadow, alleviated only by the spirit of neighbourliness and community support. Spare a thought for the  "sandwich generation" — adults  caught between caring for their own offspring and their ageing parents — often fraught with guilt due to divided loyalties and  fraying reciprocal bonds.
When financial constraints and emotional neglect mount, the final stage is often  abandonment. This rising tide of neglect precipitated the Senior Citizens Bill in 2024, which mandates legal duties and imposes penalties for abandonment.
Unlike Singapore's strict Maintenance of Parents Act, Malaysia  has historically relied on religious norms, community support and hybrid models without state enforcement. Critics warn that the Senior Citizens Bill could disrupt family harmony without addressing the structural root causes, such as economic migration.
Loneliness in old age carries  significant risks, including limited mobility, financial insecurity and  systemic barriers to healthcare. When coupled with  chronic illnesses, the danger of neglect is  silent, gradual and invisible until it inevitably degenerates into a crisis. We can no longer treat this  as a "private family matter", especially because Malaysia is on track to reach "ageing nation" status by 2030.
A coordinated national policy response is now imperative  This can be achieved by expanding community-based care, strengthening social safety nets and rethinking urban design to better accommodate the ageing and aged population.
Ultimately, Malaysians need a reminder: we will inevitably experience the vulnerabilities of ageing and its best we prepare to brace it as best we can.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Historian Sharom Ahmat dies at 86
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Historian and former Universiti Sains Malaysia (USM) administrator Sharom Ahmat has died at the age of 86.
He leaves behind his wife, Fakhitah Darus, two sons, Azmi and Azlan, and five grandchildren.
His son, Azmi, said his father was a respected academic and an accomplished university administrator.
"However, to us, he was a loving husband, father and grandfather who was deeply cherished by the family," said Azmi, who is also the Election Commission deputy chairman.
Born in September 1939, Sharom was known for his contributions as a historian and his long career in higher education, including holding several key leadership positions at USM.
He obtained a first-class honours degree in History from the University of Singapore in 1962, becoming the first Malay to achieve the distinction at the institution.
He later pursued a master's degree in American History at Brown University, Rhode Island, United States, before earning a doctorate in Southeast Asian History from the School of Oriental and African Studies, University of London, in 1969.
Sharom began his academic career at the University of Singapore in 1963 as an assistant lecturer in history before being promoted to senior lecturer and deputy dean of the Faculty of Arts and Social Sciences.
In 1973, he joined USM as a professor of history and Dean of the School of Humanities. Two years later, he was appointed the university's first Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Student Affairs), and also held senior positions in academic affairs, as well as research and development.
Former Penang chief minister Koh Tsu Koon said he first met Sharom during his tenure at USM from 1975 to 1982.
He described Sharom as soft-spoken, intellectually sharp yet humble, and always helpful, adding that he was a dedicated educator who made a significant impact.
"After leaving USM, Sharom was appointed on a pro bono basis to lead a community centre in George Town, including overseeing its development and resource distribution.
"He later became the first chief executive officer of the Wawasan Education Foundation in 2002, which also oversaw the development of Wawasan Open University and Disted College.
"His contributions to USM, Disted and WOU were immense and will always be remembered," he said.
Former Penang Heritage Trust president Choong Sim Poey said Sharom had long supported efforts to build a more united and integrated multi-ethnic society.
He said they had also collaborated on a small fund to support non-governmental organisations (NGOs) involved in education and social integration.
Another associate, Lim Mah Hui of Penang Forum, described Sharom as a role model who contributed greatly to society, praising his humility, integrity and sincerity.
Beyond his administrative roles, Sharom was also known for his academic writings, particularly on Kedah history and Kedah-Siam relations.
Funeral prayers and burial were held today between 4.30pm and 5pm at Masjid Jamek Sungai Gelugor.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Palestinian Authority: Israeli fire kills 15-year-old boy in West Bank
Article:
RAMALLAH, Palestinian Territories: Israeli troops killed a 15-year-old boy during a raid in the occupied West Bank city of Nablus on Thursday, the Palestinian Authority's health ministry said.
In a short statement, the ministry announced that "15-year-old Youssef Sameh Shtayyeh was killed by the (Israeli) occupation's gunfire in Nablus", in the north of the West Bank.
Contacted by AFP about the incident, the Israeli military said it was looking into it.
Abood al-Aker, communications director for the municipality, told AFP that six Israeli army vehicles had arrived in Nablus in the morning.
He said the soldiers spoke to shop owners in the Rafidia district's commercial area and shot the teenager as they were exiting the city.
"We don't know why they shot this kid on their way out," he said.
Israel has occupied the West Bank since 1967.
Violence has soared in the Palestinian territory since the start of the war in Gaza in October 2023.
According to an AFP tally based on Palestinian health ministry figures, Israeli troops or settlers have killed at least 1,066 Palestinians – many of them fighters, but also scores of civilians – in the West Bank since then.
Official Israeli figures say at least 46 Israelis, including soldiers and civilians, have been killed in Palestinian attacks or during Israeli military operations in the same period.-AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dzulkefly: Autism card registrations near 89,000 as awareness grows
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Registrations for the Autism Persons with Disabilities (PwD) card have risen to nearly 89,000 this year from about 23,000 in 2021, reflecting growing awareness, earlier detection and greater public acceptance, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said.
He said the almost fourfold increase showed that more parents were recognising early signs of autism and seeking professional diagnosis for their children.
Dzulkefly said that autism should not be viewed as a disease, but as a neurodevelopmental spectrum.
"When we understand this, we can begin to remove stigma. Individuals on the spectrum possess unique neurodiverse strengths and can excel in many fields, including science, the arts and technology," he said in a Facebook post in conjunction with Autism Awareness Month.
He said the approach adopted through the Health White Paper ensures continued support across a person's life, from early intervention and follow-up care to skills development and employment opportunities.
Dzulkefly said the ministry, together with the Women, Family and Community Development Ministry, would continue strengthening Community-Based Rehabilitation Centres to ensure no one is left behind.
"At the same time, we call for wider access to higher education and encourage employers to create fair, supportive and inclusive workplaces," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreign woman detained over discovery of man's body in Klang
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Police have detained a foreign woman to assist in investigations following the discovery of a man's body at a residence in Klang, near here, today.
North Klang police chief Assistant Commissioner S. Vijaya Rao said police received information on the discovery of the body of a foreign man about 3.15pm at the residence.
He said the 32-year-old woman was arrested following the discovery, and the case was being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
"The incident is believed to stem from a misunderstanding between the victim and the suspect.
"Further investigations are ongoing and the public is advised not to spread any speculation that could disrupt the investigation process or affect public order," he said in a statement today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: Cloud seeding brings significant rain to Melaka, Johor
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Cloud seeding operations (OPA) conducted in Melaka and Johor from Monday until today have generated significant rainfall in several targeted areas.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said daily rainfall of between 2mm and 24mm was recorded in Melaka, while Johor recorded between 5mm and 40mm.
Zahid, who also chairs the Central Disaster Management Committee, said the operations were carried out at eight dams and two water treatment plants in Melaka on Monday and Tuesday, before continuing in Johor yesterday and today.
"For the third series in Johor, the operation focused on key dam catchment areas, including Machap, Upper Layang, Lower Layang, Pulai and Lebam dams, as well as the Lok Heng cluster and Bukit Waha in Kota Tinggi.
"The operation was conducted by a Royal Malaysian Air Force aircraft, which took off from the Subang Air Base and was completed in under three hours, covering several identified target zones," he said in a Facebook post.
He added that favourable atmospheric conditions throughout the operation period contributed to more effective cloud formation and rainfall.
Zahid said close coordination between the Malaysian Meteorological Department, the Royal Malaysian Air Force and the National Disaster Management Agency (Nadma) played a key role in ensuring the success of the operation.
He said the initiative reflects the government's ongoing commitment to safeguarding the sustainability of national water resources, particularly amid increasingly unpredictable weather patterns.
"Insya Allah, we will continue to monitor the situation and strengthen efforts to ensure the country's preparedness and water resource management remain at the highest level," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump orders US Navy to destroy boats laying mines in Strait of Hormuz
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump on Thursday vowed the United States would destroy any vessel laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz, as he rachets up pressure on Iran to reopen the crucial sea passage.
"I have ordered the United States Navy to shoot and kill any boat, small boats though they may be... that is putting mines in the waters of the Strait of Hormuz," Trump posted.
"There is to be no hesitation. Additionally, our mine 'sweepers' are clearing the Strait right now."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global Sumud Flotilla expands as vessels converge in Syracuse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) is entering a new phase of escalation as vessels from Barcelona and across Italy converge at Marina di Siracusa in Syracuse, Sicily, on Thursday, ahead of the journey towards Gaza.
In a statement today, GSF said that with 25 additional boats joining in Italy, the flotilla now exceeds the size of last year's entire mission, signalling growing international participation and intensifying civilian pressure to challenge Israel's illegal siege on Gaza.
"With two additional ports still ahead, the mission continues to build in scale, coordination and global visibility.
"The convergence in Syracuse is both a logistical milestone and a political statement — the flotilla is not static, it is growing, adapting and advancing," it said.
GSF said the additional vessels transforms the mission into a more formidable civilian presence at sea, reflecting mounting international refusal to accept the blockade, starvation and collective punishment as normal.
The group said each new port strengthens the flotilla not only numerically but also politically, expanding its reach, deepening alliances and amplifying the global call for accountability.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MCBA foils attempt to smuggle agricultural products without permits at KLIA
Article:
SEPANG: The Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) has foiled an attempt to bring in agricultural products without valid documents at Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) Terminal 1 early today.
MCBA, in collaboration with the Malaysia Border Guard (MBG), discovered the items during an inspection at 1am involving two pieces of luggage belonging to a foreign passenger who had arrived from China.
"We found various agricultural products that did not comply with import regulations, including frozen duck meat, whole chicken and pork," it said in a statement.
"The items did not have the required import permit from the Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services Department (Maqis) or veterinary health certificates from the exporting country," it added.
The total value of the seized goods is estimated at RM1,600.
"The success highlighted the effectiveness of MBG's involvement in strengthening operational capabilities, particularly in inspection, intelligence and enforcement at the country's entry points," it said.
"The synergy between MCBA officers and MBG personnel has enhanced operational efficiency and improved detection of attempts to breach regulations," it added.
The case is being investigated under Section 11(1) of the Malaysian Quarantine and Inspection Services Act 2011 (Act 728).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 10 cats die as blaze destroys Simpang Pulai animal shelter
Article:
IPOH: Ten cats perished in a fire that destroyed an animal shelter in Simpang Pulai near here this evening.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said the department received an emergency call at 5.19pm, and a fire engine from the Simpang Pulai Fire and Rescue Station arrived at the scene about 12 minutes later.
"The fire razed a Class B building measuring 15m by 15m which was used as a cat shelter. About 80 per cent of the structure was destroyed.
"No human fatalities were reported in the incident. It is estimated that about 50 cats were at the shelter, of which 10 perished in the fire," he said in a statement today.
Sabarodzi said the fire was brought under control at 6.04pm, and the operation was concluded at 7pm.
The cause of the fire and extent of losses are still under investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: Malaysia emerges as model for disaster management
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is positioning itself as a regional hub for disaster management and humanitarian response, while emerging as a model for other countries, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said today.
Zahid, who is also National Disaster Management Agency (Nadma) chairman, said Malaysia's disaster management efforts have gained recognition from countries such as Russia, Turkiye, Myanmar and Pakistan.
"Malaysia has become an example for other countries in disaster management. Otherwise, why would teams from Russia come to learn from us? Why would Turkiye commend us?
"Myanmar acknowledged our role when it faced disasters, and Pakistan has also expressed appreciation for Nadma," he said in his keynote address at the opening of Resilient Asia 2026 at the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur (WTCKL).
Also present was Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar.
Resilient Asia 2026, organised by Nadma, is being held for the first time at WTCKL from April 24 to 26.
Zahid said the world is now living in an era where disasters are no longer rare, but increasingly part of a new normal, as climate-driven extreme weather events grow more frequent and intense, turning disasters into systemic risks rather than isolated incidents.
"In 2024 alone, global economic losses from natural disasters exceeded US$320 billion, with nearly 93 per cent attributed to weather-related events.
"But beyond the numbers, what is truly at stake is far greater — lives disrupted, communities displaced and futures left uncertain."
He said Malaysia is ready to work with more countries through joint training, knowledge-sharing and stronger collective preparedness.
"Malaysia is committed to taking on a bigger role, not just as a participant, but as a regional hub and trusted partner in disaster management and humanitarian response," he said.
Zahid said that if the region is serious about building true resilience, it must be guided by three pillars — innovation and technology, partnership and solidarity, and inclusivity through community resilience.
He said data, artificial intelligence and digital systems should be leveraged not only for innovation, but also to strengthen disaster anticipation, preparedness and response.
He added that structured regional platforms for knowledge exchange are becoming increasingly important in bringing countries together to share experiences, best practices and new approaches in disaster management.
"By expanding opportunities for joint dialogues, technical exchanges and scenario-based engagements, we can collectively raise our level of preparedness and strengthen our ability to respond effectively when it matters most," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azam orders prudent spending across MACC, urges savings on utilities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki today directed all departments and state MACC offices to practise prudent spending, including optimising the use of electricity and water.
He said fuel consumption control and the management of departmental vehicles must also be handled more efficiently.
"Global economic uncertainty due to geopolitical conflicts is placing pressure on departmental operating costs, necessitating comprehensive, prudent and disciplined cost-saving measures at all levels of the organisation," he said at the MACC monthly assembly at its headquarters today.
In his speech, Azam also urged MACC personnel to strengthen financial discipline, enhance integrity and master new technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI), in facing increasingly complex global challenges.
"The use of AI is no longer an option, but a strategic necessity in strengthening investigations and prosecutions. This technology is expected to boost productivity, expedite case resolution and enhance the organisation's overall capabilities," he said.
He also stressed the importance of all staff understanding and embracing the implementation of the Three-Pronged Approach strategy, which forms the core of the MACC Strategic Plan 2026–2030.
"This strategy encompasses effective enforcement, comprehensive corruption prevention and efficient organisational management," he said.
As part of efforts to strengthen integrity and transparency, Azam also announced that all MACC personnel are required to declare their involvement in non-governmental organisations (NGOs).
"This step is important to prevent any conflict of interest and to safeguard the organisation's reputation and credibility.
"It is a proactive measure to ensure MACC's image remains intact, especially in light of cases involving misappropriation linked to NGOs, which could have significant implications if not addressed carefully," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC officers must declare involvement in any NGO, says Azam
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: All Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission officers must declare their involvement in non-governmental organisations as part of efforts to strengthen integrity and transparency.
MACC Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the move was aimed at preventing conflicts of interest and safeguarding the commission's credibility.
"This is a proactive measure to ensure the MACC's image remains intact, especially in situations where cases involving NGO misappropriation could have significant implications if not handled carefully," he said at the commission's monthly assembly at its headquarters here today.
This comes after MACC
seized assets worth more than RM18.3 million
to assist investigations into the alleged misappropriation of funds involving an NGO.
Azam also announced that an anti-corruption-themed film, titled Konspirasi, produced by Elepfilm Entertainment Sdn Bhd with support from the MACC, will be released nationwide on May 23, as part of public education efforts on corruption.
He also reminded MACC personnel that all information provided to the media must be accurate and channelled through official MACC platforms, noting that any reporting errors could have far-reaching consequences given the sensitivity of issues involving the commission.
On a separate matter, Azam urged MACC officers and staff to strengthen financial discipline, uphold integrity and master emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence, in facing increasingly complex global challenges.
The event also saw the presentation of the MACC Best KPI Management Awards 2025 with the Public Sector Governance Division and Governance Investigation Division winning in the division category, while MACC Perlis and MACC Kedah emerged winners in the state office category.
Also present were Deputy Chief Commissioner (Operations) Datuk Seri Ahmad Khusairi Yahaya, Deputy Chief Commissioner (Prevention) Datuk Azmi Kamaruzaman, as well as senior directors and state directors of the commission.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No excuses: E-hailing platform providers told to stop delaying automatic Perkeso payments
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian e-hailing Coalition said there is no excuse for platform providers to delay the implementation of automatic social security contributions, a month after the Gig Workers Act 2025 came into force.
Its chief activist, Masrizal Mahidin, called for stricter and more punitive enforcement from the authorities to ensure full compliance from all parties, to safeguard gig workers' right to social security.
"Nearly a month after the enforcement of the Gig Workers Act 2025, on-the-ground implementation has yet to show comprehensive compliance among platform providers.
"This is despite the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) having confirmed its full readiness for the implementation of social protection for gig workers," he said.
Perkeso had assured that the "Application Programming Interface (API)" system would allow automatic and seamless contributions into Gig Workers' accounts at a rate of 1.25 per cent for each transaction as soon as a task is completed, added Masrizal.
"This means that any excuses related to technical constraints can no longer be used as a justification to delay the implementation," he said in a statement today.
Under Section 82 of the newly enforced law, gig workers have the right to have social security contributions deducted by the contracting entity on their behalf to Perkeso.
Meanwhile, Section 83 stipulates a mandatory obligation on platform providers to carry out this responsibility without exception, Masrizal said.
The
law came into force
on March 31, 2026.
Previously, Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said platforms operating Malaysia's gig economy will be given
three to six months
to integrate their systems with Perkeso.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kesedar denies allegations it failed to grant land ownership to settlers
Article:
KOTA BARU: The South Kelantan Development Authority (Kesedar) has denied claims it failed to grant land ownership to settlers, saying the process began in 2025 and has been ongoing.
Its chairman, Datuk Zawawi Othman, said efforts to transfer land ownership began shortly after he assumed the post, following instructions from Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, who is also the rural and regional development minister.
He said the signing of Form 14A — the official document for land transfer — had been implemented as early as 2025.
"I would like to clarify the claims by a Kelantan assemblyman who recently said that Kesedar had failed to grant land ownership to settlers.
"This is not a new matter. Settlers have been informed of the initiative for over a year," he said, adding that the agency's efforts go beyond land ownership.
Zawawi, who is also Kelantan Umno information chief, said Kesedar is also focusing on the next generation of settlers, including providing housing and improving livelihoods for those in need.
He said a project involving 100 residential lots under the Desa Growth Centre in Sungai Asap (Phase 1) had commenced, with an allocation of nearly RM15 million.
"Another Desa Growth Centre project in Paloh is currently at the tender stage, with an estimated cost of RM10 million," he said.
Zawawi also urged the assemblyman, Wan Rohimi Wan Daud of Pas, to focus on unresolved land ownership issues under the Kelantan government, particularly those involving resettlement schemes which predated the establishment of Kesedar.
He also called on the state government to grant land titles to Felcra participants, especially in areas such as Panggong Lalat and Bukit Tandak.
"It is unfortunate that settlers who worked the land with Felcra assistance are left empty-handed after the land was withdrawn," he said.
Zawawi added that criticisms should be balanced with accountability on all sides, rather than highlighting selective issues.
Yesterday, Menteri Besar Datuk Mohd Nassuruddin Daud had said the state government would form a special committee on land under Kesedar after Wan Rohimi had raised the issue of the alleged failure of the agency to grant land ownership to settlers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indira Gandhi’s case: Back in court today after 17 years of countless Ipoh-KL trips in quest to reunite with daughter
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 24 — Today, the High Court in Ipoh is expected to deliver its decision on whether to allow M. Indira Gandhi to start contempt of court proceedings against the Inspector-General of Police (IGP).
Indira told
Malay Mail
: “I don’t know how it’s going to turn out and I always cross my fingers that justice always prevails.”
Indira’s main aim, however, is not to get the police to be held in contempt of court, but to find her daughter Prasana Diksa — just like with all her other court cases.
“It’s not that we want to blame the police, but if they could do their job better and if they have at least found out something, it would have been more encouraging for me.
“But then, we don’t see the police are doing their work, that is what disappoints us. Malaysia’s police are a very fantastic force, they could find any criminals, within hours also they can do.
“But why is the silence in this case and that 17 years — it’s like [it] fell into a deaf ear. So that is really disappointing us,” she added.
This is Indira’s second attempt to cite the IGP for contempt. She filed it on November 17 last year and the High Court heard her application on February 27.
Indira’s contempt of court application revolves around the police’s alleged continued failure to enforce the High Court’s May 30, 2014 court orders to recover her daughter and to arrest her ex-husband K. Pathmanathan.
Indira’s lawyer Rajesh Nagarajan explained: “What is new now is: i) the prolonged failure to act despite a valid court order; ii) information suggesting that Pathmanathan is openly accessing government aid (Budi95, Sara 100).
“This raises serious concerns that: his whereabouts are traceable, and the authorities have the means but have intentionally failed to act.”
If the High Court decides today to allow the contempt of court proceedings to start, the court will hear the case and then decide whether the IGP has committed contempt of court, Rajesh added.
When asked about the purpose of this contempt application, Rajesh said: “It is another legal avenue to exert pressure on the authorities to secure Prasana’s recovery.”
“A successful contempt application would: i) increase pressure on the authorities, and ii) potentially compel focused efforts to recover the child,” he said.
Indira, seen together with a teddy bear that belonged to Prasana as a baby, walked in the ‘March for Justice’ from Sogo Kuala Lumpur to Bukit Aman in November 2025 to get the Inspector-General of Police’s help to recover Prasana. — Picture by Raymond Manuel
Indira was 34 years old in 2009, when her marriage broke down and her Hindu-turned-Muslim husband K. Pathmanathan snatched away her youngest daughter Prasana Diksa — who was not even one year old then.
Since then, Indira said she has been to court “countless” times over the last 17 years, all with the hope of reuniting with her daughter.
“I think half of my life I have spent in all the courts,” Indira, now 51, said when contacted ahead of the court decision today in Ipoh.
Indira has been to the courts in Ipoh (without missing any in Ipoh), Kuala Lumpur and also Putrajaya, while juggling work and raising her two other children.
“It was not easy at all, I mean, as a single mother providing for the other older children, and then most of the time I use public transport — either I go by train or by bus,” she said.
While saying that many think she stays in Kuala Lumpur, Indira said she actually travels all the way from Ipoh — either about 2.5 hours by train or about three hours by bus as these options are cheaper — one day in advance for all the court cases in KL and Putrajaya.
“Usually I will travel one day ahead because the proceedings are like 9 o’clock in the morning, so I have to be there by 8 o’clock. So if I take the 5.30am train, I’m scared I might miss, so I have to come a day earlier,” she said, adding that she sometimes stays at her sister’s place in Kajang to cut costs.
Indira is seen here with her mother (left), and her then lawyer M. Kulasegaran (right) after winning a case at the Federal Court on January 29, 2018. — Picture by Zuraneeza Zulkifli
Indira is sometimes accompanied by her mother — who is now 84 years old — to court, and also occasionally by her older daughter Tevi Darsiny to the courts in Putrajaya.
She said she had on many occasions taken up her previous lawyer M. Kulasegaran’s offer to drive her to KL and Putrajaya for court when he was still handling her cases.
Indira worked as a kindergarten teacher in Ipoh for many years but had to give up this job to become a full-time tutor for primary school students some years ago.
“The main thing I resigned from being a kindergarten teacher is because of all this — because I couldn’t take frequent leave for my court proceedings. So my only option is to be a freelancer, so I started being a tutor, because I can manage my timetable,” she said.
Indira said she was not only teaching then, but also baking and offering transportation or carpooling services to send students to school just to earn extra money.
“Of course my siblings do help me, but then everything still comes back to me to give moral support, to be there for their every school function. So juggling with work, and court cases, with children, it’s not easy,” she said, adding that she tried to make sure to be there for her children’s main school activities.
Karan Dinish and Tevi Darsiny, aged 19 and 20 then in 2017, with their mother M. Indira Gandhi and the only photo they have of Prasana Diksa as a baby. — Picture by Farhan Najib
Thankful for all the support from public, lawyers
Indira said she received donations and support from the public, adding that she is “very grateful” that many understood and “took this as not a religious issue”, but as a “mother’s concern to find her daughter”.
Indira confirmed that she did not have to pay legal fees to all the lawyers that have been representing her — including Kulasegaran up until he became a minister — as they have been doing so pro bono even until now.
“He has helped me in so many ways, not only for my case, even for my children’s education and financially also he has helped us.
“So I’m really so grateful for what YB has done, I think without him, we might not also have won the Federal Court cases,” she said of Kulasegaran, who is also Ipoh Barat MP and now deputy minister for law and institutional reform.
She described her current pro bono lawyers from the law firm Raj & Sach as being good lawyers who are “gems” and who have helped her in many ways.
“As I know, yes, it is very expensive, and just imagine, 17 years the case is still going on — the amount of work… I don’t know how much it cost them, but I know it is quite a lot.”
Indira’s current lawyer Rajesh confirmed that he began representing her around 2019 in her legal efforts to be reunited with her daughter Prasana on a pro bono basis.
Rajesh said he had attended court at least 30 to 40 times at all levels — High Court, Court of Appeal, Federal Court — over the years for Indira’s cases.
“Our sole purpose has always been to reunite her with her child. This is not just a private dispute — it raises serious issues about enforcement of court orders and the rule of law,” he said.
M. Indira Gandhi (2nd right) with her children Karan Dinish (2nd left) and Tevi Darsiny (far left), lawyer Rajesh Nagarajan (far right) and Indira Gandhi Action Team (Ingat) chief Arun Dorasamy (centre) at the High Court in Ipoh April 6, 2026. — Picture by John Bunyan
What are the court cases being pursued by Indira now?
Earlier this month, Indira and her two older children went to the High Court in Ipoh, hoping to see Prasana ahead of her April 8 birthday.
But Prasana’s location remains unknown until today, with Indira saying she “just offered a prayer at the temple” to mark her daughter’s 18th birthday.
The High Court in Ipoh has scheduled June 25 as the next date for the police to update the court on its progress in finding Indira’s ex-husband and executing the arrest warrant on him — which Indira hopes will lead to answers about Prasana.
